name: Silver Cloud Mine
short_description: Historic silver and gold prospect on BLM land near Elko. Explore gentle slopes at 5,346 ft elevation with opportunities to find quartz veins and mineral specimens.
description:
Silver Cloud Mine is a historic silver and gold prospect located on public BLM land in Elko County, Nevada. The site sits at 5,346 feet elevation on gently sloping terrain that is relatively accessible to rockhounders of various skill levels. The area offers opportunities to collect silver and gold-bearing specimens, along with quartz and other minerals associated with historic mining activity.

The geology of this region reflects Nevada's position within the Basin and Range province, where Tertiary-age mineralization created widespread silver and gold deposits. Hydrothermal fluids circulating through fractured bedrock deposited precious metals and quartz veins over millions of years. The Silver Cloud area was historically prospected and mined, leaving exposed mineralized zones and quartz outcroppings that rockhounders can still access today.

Visitors should expect rocky terrain with scattered mineralized material. The gentle slopes make this site more accessible than many high-elevation Nevada prospects, though sturdy footwear is still recommended. The nearby wash areas and hillside exposures are the most productive zones for finding specimens. This is a popular destination for rockhounders seeking authentic Nevada mining country without requiring extreme technical skill or permits.

As with all BLM land, personal use collection is permitted under standard Forest Land Policy and Management Act guidelines. The site is best visited during fall and winter months when temperatures are mild and conditions favor outdoor exploration. Early morning visits provide better lighting for spotting minerals and allow you to cover more ground before heat or darkness becomes a factor.
history: This area lies within Nevada's prolific silver and gold belt, where Oligocene and Miocene hydrothermal systems deposited silver, gold, and quartz veins along fault zones and in fractured bedrock. The Silver Cloud area was actively prospected and mined during Nevada's mining booms, with exploratory work exposing mineralized quartz veins and precious metal deposits that remain accessible to rockhounders today.
tips: Search the exposed hillsides and small wash areas for quartz veins, which often contain visible silver and gold flakes or wire silver. Look for white to gray quartz outcroppings mixed with decomposed granite and oxidized iron-stained rock, which indicates past hydrothermal activity. Bring a 10x loupe and UV light to spot fluorescent minerals and fine crystalline detail. The gentle terrain means you can cover significant ground, but stay alert for loose rock on slopes and respect any old mine workings by keeping a safe distance from deep shafts or unstable areas.
terrain_notes: Elevation 5,346 feet on gently sloping terrain with scattered exposed mineralized zones. Sparse to moderate desert shrub vegetation typical of Great Basin high desert. Parking area sits on or near unpaved BLM access road. Walking distances from parking to productive collecting areas range from 0.25 to 1 mile over uneven rocky ground with variable footing.
best_season: October - April. Higher elevation and desert location make summer temperatures extreme; fall and winter provide comfortable collecting conditions and better visibility for spotting minerals.
written_directions: From Elko, drive north on US 93 for approximately 18 miles. Turn east onto local BLM/dirt access road (check current BLM maps for exact road designation and conditions). Follow the road for approximately 4-6 miles until you reach the Silver Cloud prospect area near the coordinates. A high-clearance vehicle is recommended due to rough road conditions. GPS navigation to 41.05277, -116.624842 is strongly advised.
road_conditions: Rough unpaved BLM dirt road, typically passable in dry conditions with high-clearance vehicle. Washboard and ruts common. Impassable during wet weather.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and cleared areas along BLM access road near the prospect site. No developed parking lot.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Respect historic mine structures and do not enter abandoned mine shafts or adits. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use only - no commercial collecting
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
Do not enter or excavate abandoned mine workings
Pack out all trash
Respect surrounding vegetation and stay on established routes when possible
nearest_services: Elko, Nevada, approximately 20-25 miles south. Full services including gas, food, water, and lodging available in town.
accessibility_notes: Gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers with basic fitness. Rough unpaved road to area and uneven rocky ground at site. No wheelchair accessibility. Sturdy closed-toe footwear required. Not suitable for limited mobility or those unfamiliar with remote backcountry travel.

faq.question: What can you find rockhounding at Silver Cloud Mine near Elko, Nevada?
faq.answer: Silver Cloud Mine offers opportunities to collect silver, gold, and quartz specimens. The site is a historic silver and gold prospect where mineralized quartz veins are exposed on hillsides and wash areas. You may find wire silver, gold flakes, and well-formed quartz crystals with associated minerals typical of Nevada's Basin and Range hydrothermal deposits.

faq.question: Is Silver Cloud Mine open to rockhounding without a permit?
faq.answer: Yes. Silver Cloud Mine sits on public BLM land where personal use rockhounding is permitted without a permit under FLPMA regulations. You are allowed to collect up to 25 lbs per day for personal use only. No commercial collection or motorized excavation is permitted.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Silver Cloud Mine?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. The site sits at 5,346 feet elevation in the Great Basin, where summer temperatures become extreme and uncomfortable for outdoor collecting. Fall and winter provide mild temperatures and better visibility for spotting minerals.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Silver Cloud Mine?
faq.answer: Bring a 10x loupe to examine specimens for precious metal content and crystal detail, a UV light to identify fluorescent minerals, a small hammer or pick for extracting samples, and a collection bag. Sturdy closed-toe hiking boots are essential on the rocky terrain. A GPS device or smartphone with offline maps is recommended since cell service is spotty.

faq.question: Is Silver Cloud Mine in Nevada suitable for beginner rockhounders?
faq.answer: Yes, the gentle slopes and exposed mineralized zones make this site beginner-friendly. No technical climbing or extreme hiking is required. However, the remote location and rough access road mean you should come prepared with supplies, extra water, and a vehicle suitable for rough dirt roads. Always inform someone of your trip plans.

faq.question: Can I take my dog rockhounding at Silver Cloud Mine?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are allowed on BLM land. Keep your dog under control, bring plenty of water for both you and your pet, and watch for hazards like loose rock, abandoned mine shafts, and desert wildlife. The remote location means help is far away, so exercise caution and ensure your dog has a GPS tracker or collar.

meta_description: Find silver, gold, and quartz at Silver Cloud Mine near Elko, NV. Free BLM rockhounding with no permit required. Best October - April.
